masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through World Flags
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through World Flags
masthead.dek: Thirty-seven questions that route a flag's quirk to the country flying it. Each leans on a discriminator, not raw recall — which of the near-twin red-and-white flags puts white on top? Which lone flag isn't even a rectangle? Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Thirty-seven questions verified to Wikipedia, Britannica, WorldAtlas, and the CIA World Factbook. Several famous 'only flag that…' claims are framed as paired discriminators because the simple 'only' is contested.

plan.note: Answers route a flag detail to a nameable country (or a color). Look-alike flags are framed as 'which of the twins'; contested 'only' claims are split by a confirming detail so the reasoning, not a false absolute, carries the question.

questions.q: Almost every country flies a four-sided flag. One country's flag is not four-sided at all — it's two stacked triangular pennants. Which country?
questions.detail: Nepal's flag is two stacked crimson pennants with blue borders — the only non-quadrilateral national flag.
questions.why: The reflex is 'square' flags like Switzerland, but those are still four-sided; only Nepal breaks the quadrilateral.
questions.more: Its proportions are fixed by a construction formula in the constitution.

questions.q: It is the only national flag that shows a modern assault rifle — an AK-47 with a bayonet — crossed over a hoe on an open book. Which country?
questions.detail: Mozambique's flag shows an AK-47, a hoe, and an open book for defense, production, and study.
questions.why: Other flags carry swords or spears; only one has a 20th-century firearm.
questions.more: Proposals to remove the rifle have been rejected by parliament.

questions.cat: World flags · people as main feature
questions.q: Two men — one holding an axe, one a paddle — stand on either side of the coat of arms at the center of this flag, making it the only flag with people as a main feature. Which country?
questions.detail: Belize's flag shows a logger and a mariner flanking a shield, with the motto 'Sub Umbra Floreo.'
questions.why: Saints appear as small badges elsewhere; Belize is the only flag with humans as a central element.
questions.more: The axe nods to logging origins, the paddle to the coast.

questions.q: Only two national flags contain any of this color — one hides it in a parrot's feathers, the other in a rainbow. Historically it was the rarest, costliest dye. Which color?
questions.detail: Purple appears only on Dominica's flag (the Sisserou parrot) and Nicaragua's (a rainbow band).
questions.why: Purple feels common, but its historic dye scarcity kept it off flags.
questions.more: Tyrian purple was so expensive it was reserved for royalty.

questions.q: Its flag shows the whole island's copper-colored outline above two olive branches — one of only two flags that put the country's own map front and center. Which country?
questions.detail: Cyprus's flag depicts the island in copper-orange (for its copper ore) with olive branches for peace.
questions.why: Kosovo also shows its map, so the copper-island + olive-branch detail separates the two.
questions.more: The copper ties to the metal that named the island.

questions.cat: World flags · different front and back
questions.q: This is the only national flag whose front and back show different emblems — one side bears the national coat of arms, the other the treasury seal. Which country?
questions.detail: Paraguay's flag carries the coat of arms on the obverse and the treasury seal on the reverse.
questions.why: Saudi Arabia's reverse only duplicates text; Paraguay uses two different emblems.
questions.more: The reverse seal reads 'Peace and Justice.'

questions.q: People often guess Brazil for the flag with the most stars, but Brazil's 27 lose to another country's 50. Which country actually has the most stars?
questions.detail: The U.S. flag has 50 stars, one per state — more than any other national flag.
questions.why: Brazil's starry sky makes it the reflex answer, but the U.S. nearly doubles its count.
questions.more: Brazil is second with 27.

questions.cat: World flags · stars are a real sky
questions.q: The stars on this country's flag are not decorative — they reproduce the exact night sky over its old capital at the moment it became a republic. Which country?
questions.detail: Brazil's 27 stars show the sky over Rio de Janeiro on the morning of November 15, 1889.
questions.why: Most starred flags use tidy rows; Brazil fixes them to a real astronomical moment.
questions.more: The motto 'Ordem e Progresso' comes from Auguste Comte.

questions.q: A dragon appears on only two well-known flags. The sovereign UN country flies a WHITE dragon clutching jewels across a diagonal yellow-and-orange field. Which country?
questions.detail: Bhutan's flag shows Druk, the white Thunder Dragon, holding jewels over a yellow and orange field.
questions.why: Wales also flies a dragon, but it's red and not a sovereign UN state.
questions.more: Bhutan's name means 'Land of the Thunder Dragon.'

questions.q: A few flags show a building, but only this one depicts a famous temple complex — a white silhouette of it sits on the red center band. Which country?
questions.detail: Cambodia's flag shows a white Angkor Wat on a red band between two blue stripes.
questions.why: Portugal and Spain show towers/castles; the named temple is the discriminator.
questions.more: Angkor Wat has been on Cambodian flags since the 19th century.

questions.q: Almost every flag is wider than it is tall, but only this country's flag is more than twice as wide as it is high — an 11-by-28 stretch with a serrated white band. Which country?
questions.detail: Qatar's flag has an 11:28 ratio (maroon with a white serrated band), the most elongated national flag.
questions.why: Switzerland and Vatican pull the other way as squares; Qatar is the extreme-wide outlier.
questions.more: Its maroon came from a dye reaction that darkened the original red.

questions.cat: World flags · six colors, no seal
questions.q: With a Y-shaped split design, this is the only national flag built from six colors and no seal or emblem. Which country?
questions.detail: South Africa's flag uses red, blue, green, black, yellow, and white in a horizontal-Y layout, adopted in 1994.
questions.why: Most multicolor flags top out at four or five; the six-color seal-free design is unique.
questions.more: It first flew at Nelson Mandela's 1994 inauguration.

questions.q: Its flag is a single off-center golden disc on a plain blue field — but unlike the look-alike sun flags, this disc is a full moon, prized for fishing and planting. Which country?
questions.detail: Palau's flag is a yellow full-moon disc, set toward the hoist, on a sky-blue field.
questions.why: Japan (centered red sun) and Bangladesh (off-center red sun) are the reflex answers; the moon-on-blue isolates Palau.
questions.more: The full moon is the best time for Palauan fishing.

questions.q: Because its green flag carries a sacred declaration of faith above a sword, this country never lowers its flag to half-mast, even in mourning. Which country?
questions.detail: Saudi Arabia's flag bears the shahada and a sword; lowering it is considered improper.
questions.why: The reasoning is the religious text itself — a flag with scripture can't be lowered.
questions.more: The text is mirrored so it reads correctly on both sides.

questions.q: A single green tree sits dead-center on this red-white-red flag, chosen for its biblical fame and longevity. Which country?
questions.detail: Lebanon's flag centers a green cedar of Lebanon on white between two red bands.
questions.why: Canada's central plant is a leaf, not a whole tree; the cedar pins it to Lebanon.
questions.more: The cedar is referenced repeatedly in the Bible.

questions.cat: World flags · black eagle on red
questions.q: A two-headed eagle appears on several Balkan emblems, but only this country flies a black double-headed eagle alone on a plain red field as its whole flag. Which country?
questions.detail: Albania's flag is a black double-headed eagle on red, tied to the hero Skanderbeg.
questions.why: Montenegro (gold eagle on a shield) and Serbia (white eagle) also use the motif; the bare black eagle is Albania.
questions.more: Albanians call their country 'Shqiperia,' often linked to the eagle.

questions.q: A red-and-black flag with the Southern Cross and a golden bird-of-paradise was designed by a 15-year-old in a national contest. Which country?
questions.detail: PNG's flag pairs the Southern Cross with a Raggiana bird-of-paradise; Susan Karike designed it at 15.
questions.why: The bird-of-paradise is the discriminator versus other Southern-Cross flags.
questions.more: Red and black are traditional colors of many PNG tribes.

questions.cat: World flags · lion with a sword
questions.q: Its flag shows a golden lion gripping a sword, framed by four sacred fig leaves in the corners. Which country?
questions.detail: Sri Lanka's flag shows a sword-bearing lion on maroon with four bo leaves at the corners.
questions.why: A lion with a sword plus religious leaf corners is a unique combination.
questions.more: The corner symbols were changed from spearheads to bo leaves in 1972.

questions.q: Its central emblem shows an eagle perched on a cactus devouring a snake — the exact omen a people were told to seek when founding their capital city. Which country?
questions.detail: Mexico's flag centers an eagle on a prickly-pear cactus eating a serpent, marking the founding of Tenochtitlan.
questions.why: The legend-to-emblem link plus the cactus-and-snake detail rules out other eagle flags.
questions.more: The eagle stood for the sun god Huitzilopochtli.

questions.cat: World flags · white over red twin
questions.q: Three countries fly plain red-and-white two-stripe flags so alike they cause mix-ups — but only one puts WHITE on top of RED. Which country?
questions.detail: Poland's flag is white over red; Indonesia and Monaco are red over white.
questions.why: Indonesia and Monaco are the red-over-white twins; Poland is the lone reversed one.
questions.more: Indonesia and Monaco differ only in aspect ratio.

questions.cat: World flags · oldest in continuous use
questions.q: A white off-center cross on red, this is recognized as the oldest national flag still in continuous use — and the template every other Nordic flag copied. Which country?
questions.detail: Denmark's Dannebrog holds the record as the oldest continuously used national flag.
questions.why: Several Nordic flags share the offset cross, but Denmark is the original.
questions.more: Legend ties its origin to a 1219 battle.

questions.q: Its flag shows a golden frigatebird soaring over a rising sun, above three wavy white stripes for the ocean and its three island groups. Which country?
questions.detail: Kiribati's flag has a gold frigatebird and 17-ray sun on red over blue water with three white waves.
questions.why: The frigatebird-over-sun-and-waves scene is distinctive among Pacific flags.
questions.more: The 17 rays stand for the 16 Gilbert Islands plus Banaba.

questions.cat: World flags · square flag with a cross
questions.q: Only two countries fly a square flag. The one that is NOT the Vatican shows a bold white cross on red. Which country?
questions.detail: Switzerland's flag is a square red field with a centered white cross.
questions.why: Vatican City is the other square flag, so the white-cross detail separates them.
questions.more: The Red Cross emblem is the Swiss flag with colors reversed.

questions.q: On this green flag the off-center red disc stands for the blood of those lost in a liberation war, and the green for fertile land — not for Islam. Which country?
questions.detail: Bangladesh's flag is a red disc on green; the disc is offset so it looks centered when waving.
questions.why: Many assume green always means Islam; here it means the land.
questions.more: The disc represents the rising sun and the war dead.

questions.q: Its red flag with a white crescent and star comes from an empire that adopted the symbol after conquering Constantinople — and is why many later read the crescent-and-star as Islamic. Which country?
questions.detail: Turkey's flag descends from Ottoman use; the symbol predates Islam but spread through Ottoman influence.
questions.why: Many flags use the crescent-and-star; Turkey is the historical source that popularized it.
questions.more: The symbol traces back to Central Asian and Mesopotamian sky worship.

questions.cat: World flags · parrot and ten stars
questions.q: A green parrot ringed by ten green stars sits on a cross-divided field of green, yellow, and black on this Caribbean island's flag. Which country?
questions.detail: Dominica's flag centers the Sisserou parrot inside ten stars on a tricolor cross.
questions.why: Nicaragua is the other purple-bearing flag, but the parrot-and-ten-stars isolates Dominica.
questions.more: The Sisserou parrot is found only on Dominica.

questions.q: Its flag centers a single stylized leaf between two red bars — and despite looking ornate, that leaf has exactly eleven points. Which country?
questions.detail: Canada's flag centers an 11-point red maple leaf on white between two red bands.
questions.why: People assume the point count matches provinces; the 11 points were chosen to look clean when waving.
questions.more: The point count carries no official symbolic meaning.

questions.cat: World flags · square flag with keys
questions.q: The other square flag besides Switzerland splits into yellow and white halves with crossed keys and a papal tiara. Which sovereign state?
questions.detail: Vatican City's square flag has gold and white bands with the crossed keys of St. Peter and the papal tiara.
questions.why: Switzerland is the other square flag, so the keys-and-tiara detail separates them.
questions.more: It was adopted in 1929 with the Lateran Treaty.

questions.q: A tiny European principality has a red-over-white flag nearly identical to a huge Southeast Asian nation's. Which is the small European one?
questions.detail: Monaco (4:5) and Indonesia (2:3) share red-over-white bands; Monaco's predates Indonesia's national adoption.
questions.why: The slicer is which one is the small European principality, not the giant archipelago.
questions.more: Indonesia kept its flag despite Monaco's objection, citing older origins.

questions.q: A Central African country has blue-yellow-red vertical stripes almost indistinguishable from a European country's flag. Which is the African one?
questions.detail: Chad and Romania share blue-yellow-red vertical bands, differing only in a slightly darker blue.
questions.why: The reasoning is sorting the African member from the European one.
questions.more: The pair is often called the most identical of any two national flags.

questions.cat: World flags · readopted a pre-coup flag
questions.q: Which country brought back its pre-1969 red-black-green crescent flag in 2011 after an uprising toppled a long-ruling leader?
questions.detail: After Gaddafi's fall in 2011, Libya readopted the Kingdom-era flag it had used before his 1969 coup.
questions.why: The reasoning chains a 2011 revolution to a revived monarchy-era flag.
questions.more: It replaced the plain green flag Gaddafi introduced in 1977.

questions.cat: World flags · founded by freed slaves
questions.q: Which African country, founded by freed American slaves, has a flag of red and white stripes with a single white star in a blue field?
questions.detail: Liberia's flag has 11 stripes for its 11 declaration signers and one star, modeled on the U.S. flag.
questions.why: The U.S.-like design is the clue; the logic is the only nation settled by freed American slaves.
questions.more: It was hand-stitched by a committee of seven women in 1847.

questions.cat: World flags · inverted to signal war
questions.q: Which country's flag is officially turned upside down, with the red stripe on top, to signal that the nation is at war?
questions.detail: In peacetime the blue band is up; flipping it so red is up officially signals a state of war.
questions.why: Inverting a flag usually means distress; here it's a built-in, legal wartime mode.
questions.more: The custom dates to revolutionaries in the late 1800s.

questions.q: Which country has the most recently changed national flag, after it added red stripes above and below its green field in 2017?
questions.detail: Mauritania added red top and bottom stripes in 2017 to honor those who died for independence.
questions.why: Several flags changed in the 2010s; the slicer is the 2017 redesign, the newest of the lot.
questions.more: The crescent-and-star green field was kept.

questions.cat: World flags · no red, white, or blue
questions.q: Which Caribbean country's flag, a gold diagonal cross over green and black, uses none of the three most common flag colors — red, white, or blue?
questions.detail: Jamaica's gold saltire with green and black is the most-cited flag using none of red, white, or blue.
questions.why: Almost every flag uses at least one of those three.
questions.more: Gold for sunlight, green for the land, black for the people's strength.

questions.q: Which African country changed its flag to a full sun in 2010, then reverted to its original rising-sun flag just two years later?
questions.detail: Malawi adopted a full white sun in 2010 but restored the rising-sun flag in 2012.
questions.why: A flag change that gets undone is unusual; reasoning lands on the southern African state that flip-flopped.
questions.more: The rising sun symbolizes the dawn of hope across Africa.

questions.cat: World flags · absent from a union flag
questions.q: Which part of the United Kingdom, despite its famous red dragon, has no symbol represented in the Union Jack?
questions.detail: Wales is not in the Union Jack because it was already joined to England when the flag was created in 1606.
questions.why: People assume all four home nations appear; the 1536/1606 timing reveals Wales is missing.
questions.more: Petitions to add the Welsh dragon still surface.
questions.source: Historic UK (Wales and the Union Flag).
